notes.anchor: more ample than his own, than Africanus's
notes.note: Cicero compares the flattering language of Caesar's published letter to the way the elder Scipio Aemilianus (Africanus, conqueror of Carthage in 146 BC) was praised; the point is that Caesar lavished on a correspondent praise grander than any owed even to that paragon.

notes.anchor: went out to the fifth milestone
notes.note: Distances from Rome were reckoned by numbered milestones along the great roads; going out as far as the fifth milestone to meet someone was a marked public honour, here a show of welcome that Cicero finds politically compromising.

Manius Aemilius Lepidus, consul of 66 BC, an elderly consular who was sitting out the war near Cicero at Formiae and keeping him company; not to be confused with the praetor of 49 and future triumvir.

notes.anchor: all this clemency is being heaped up toward the Cinnan kind of cruelty
notes.note: L. Cornelius Cinna dominated Rome in the 80s BC and was remembered for the bloody Marian terror; Cicero fears Caesar's much-advertised clementia is only a prelude to violence on Cinna's model.

notes.anchor: the younger Balbus came to me
notes.note: The "younger Balbus" is the nephew of L. Cornelius Balbus of Gades, Caesar's chief agent and Cicero's correspondent; he was carrying Caesar's overtures to the consul Lentulus, while the "elder Balbus" wrote separately to reassure Cicero.

notes.anchor: On the sixth day before the Kalends, in the evening
notes.note: The Roman calendar counted days inclusively backward from the Kalends (the first of the month); here the sixth and fifth days before the Kalends of March fall on 24 and 25 February 49 BC.
